perturbation.original_text: shall mean any company controlling, controlled by, or under common control with a Party to this Agreement, cont rol meaning in this context the direct or indirect ownership of more than fifty percent (50%) of the voting stock/shares of a company, or the power to nominate more than half of the directors, or the power otherwise to determine the policy of a company or organi sation.
perturbation.changed_text: shall mean any company influencing, influenced by, or with some association with a Party to this Agreement, infl uence meaning in this context any level of direct or indirect connection, including but not limited to ownership, investment, partnership, or familial relationship. The threshold for influence will be determined at the discretion of each party.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Affiliate' is crucial for determining the scope of confidentiality. By replacing 'controlling, controlled by, or under common control' with the vague 'influencing, influenced by, or some association with', and removing the specific ownership threshold, the contract creates ambiguity. Defining influence at each party's discretion contradicts established legal definitions of corporate affiliation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and its related rules, particularly Rule 12b-2, provide specific definitions of 'control' in the context of securities regulations. The modified text's definition of 'influence' lacks the specificity required by securities regulations, potentially leading to misinterpretations and non-compliance if the contract involves entities subject to these regulations. The rule defines control as “possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.”

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The modification significantly weakens the definition of 'Affiliate', replacing specific control criteria with a vague 'influence' standard to be determined at each party's discretion. This contradicts the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 which, according to the Law Explanation, requires specificity. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.12b-2, implies that the definition of terms like 'control' have specified legal meanings which the modified text ignores. The change creates a contradiction because it replaces a defined legal standard with a subjective and discretionary one.

perturbation.original_text: All information disclosed by a Party or by Affiliate s of a Party to the other Party or its respective Affiliates orally, electronically, writing or by any other means during the data sharing negotiations shall be considered as confidential unless expressly stated otherwise by the disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Some information disclosed by a Party to the other Party orally, electronically, writing or by any other means during the data sharing negotiations may be considered as confidential at the discretion of the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a significant ambiguity regarding what constitutes 'confidential information.' By making confidentiality discretionary, it contradicts the core purpose of a confidentiality agreement, which is to clearly define and protect sensitive information. This is a drastic departure from industry norms and legal expectations surrounding NDAs.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) defines 'trade secret' and implies a reasonable expectation of confidentiality for information to be considered a trade secret. By making confidentiality discretionary, the modified text undermines the ability to claim trade secret protection under the UTSA, as it removes the element of reasonable expectation of confidentiality, which is part of having a trade secret.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a significant ambiguity regarding what constitutes 'confidential information' by making confidentiality discretionary. This contradicts the core purpose of a confidentiality agreement, which is to clearly define and protect sensitive information. The Law Explanation states this undermines the ability to claim trade secret protection under the UTSA, as it removes the element of reasonable expectation of confidentiality. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the explanation and comparison between original and changed text reveals that making confidentiality discretionary directly opposes the normal expectation of confidentiality necessary for information to be considered a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving Party undertakes not to use the Information disclosed to it for any purpose except the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving Party undertakes to primarily use the Information disclosed to it for the Purpose, but may use the information for any other business operation to improve business efficiency.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'but may use the information for any other business operation to improve business efficiency,' the change broadens the permissible use of confidential information beyond the stated 'Purpose.' This directly conflicts with the fundamental principle of a confidentiality agreement, which is to restrict the use of disclosed information to a specific, agreed-upon purpose.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, 18 U.S.C. § 1836
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Using confidential information for purposes beyond the agreed-upon 'Purpose' could be considered misappropriation under the DTSA, especially if it involves commercial gain, as stated in the changed text 'to improve business efficiency'. DTSA defines misappropriation as “acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.”

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by allowing the receiving party to use the confidential information for 'any other business operation to improve business efficiency,' which broadens the scope beyond the original 'Purpose.' The LLM explanation cites the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) and defines misappropriation as 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By allowing use beyond the agreed purpose, the modified contract potentially permits misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings related to violations, suggesting legal action can be taken against such misappropriation. The modified text weakens the original restriction and could lead to a violation of trade secret law, making the contradiction direct and significant.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of [ … ] , without regard to any principle of conflict or choice of laws that would cause the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the principles of equity, regardless of jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing a specific jurisdiction's laws with 'principles of equity' creates significant uncertainty. While equity principles can supplement law, they cannot replace statutory and common law. 'Principles of equity' is a vague term that will be interpreted differently by different people. This change makes the contract unenforceable because it fails to specify a body of law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws addresses choice-of-law provisions in contracts. Section 187 deals with the law chosen by the parties. While parties have some autonomy in choosing the governing law, choosing a non-legal framework like 'principles of equity' is unlikely to be upheld by courts, as it lacks the established legal rules and precedents necessary for predictable enforcement. This defeats the purpose of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies governance by the laws of a particular jurisdiction. The changed text replaces this with 'principles of equity, regardless of jurisdiction'. The Law Explanation states that choosing a non-legal framework like 'principles of equity' is unlikely to be upheld because it lacks established legal rules. The Perturbation Explanation states that this replacement creates uncertainty and makes the contract unenforceable. While the provided snippets are unhelpful, the combined explanations highlight that replacing established legal framework with principles of equity constitutes a contradiction because it undermines the enforceability of the contract within a defined legal system, as described in the Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be valid when signed by duly authorised representatives of th e Parties and shall be binding on each Party for 10 (ten) years as from the date of signature of the last signatory, even if at the end of the negotiations a data sharing agreement is not signed between the Parties, or until such time as the Information enters into the public domain.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be valid when initialed by representatives of th e Parties and shall be binding on each Party for a period deemed appropriate by the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'signed by duly authorized representatives' to 'initialed by representatives' and replacing a fixed 10-year term with a period 'deemed appropriate by the disclosing party' introduces ambiguity and potential unenforceability. Initialing might not meet the legal requirement for a valid signature in many jurisdictions, especially for contracts of significant duration. Furthermore, allowing the disclosing party to unilaterally determine the duration contradicts the principle of mutual agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds (varies by state, e.g., California Civil Code § 1624)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds, as exemplified by California Civil Code § 1624, requires certain contracts, particularly those that cannot be performed within one year, to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. The modification creates a scenario where the contract might not be properly signed (initialed instead of signed) and the duration is uncertain, potentially rendering it unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds if the contract's subject matter falls within its scope.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Code, Civil Code - CIV § 1624 | FindLaw California Code, Civil Code - CIV § 1624 Current as of January 01, 2023 | Updated by FindLaw Staff (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party's agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794 . (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period than one year, for compensation or a commission. (5) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed during the lifetime of the promisor. (6) An agreement by a purchaser of real property to pay an indebtedness secured by a mortgage or deed of trust upon the property purchased, unless assumption of the indebtedness by the purchaser is specifically provided for in the conveyance of the property. (7) A contract, promise, undertaking, or commitment to loan money or to grant or extend credit, in an amount greater than one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000), not primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, made by a person engaged in the business of lending or arranging for the lending of money or extending credit. For purposes of this section, a contract, promise, undertaking , or commitment to loan money secured solely by residential
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 1624, which is provided in both scraped snippets. Specifically, § 1624(a)(1) states that agreements not to be performed within a year must be in writing and 'subscribed by the party to be charged'. The changed text replaces 'signed by duly authorized representatives' with 'initialed by representatives', which potentially fails to meet the 'subscribed' requirement for contracts lasting longer than a year. Further, the original text specified a 10-year term, while the changed text allows the duration to be determined by the disclosing party, creating uncertainty and potentially rendering the contract unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds as outlined in § 1624(a)(1) if it is deemed to last longer than one year.
